When combined with the fund currently being slightly above its own long-term average price, we can conclude that it might be possible for investors to get a better price by waiting until the discount widens slightly. Honestly, though, the fund’s shares are still trading well below their intrinsic value, so the current price is probably fine if you want to add the fund to a portfolio today.

Expenses

The website for the LMP Capital and Income Fund states that the fund has an expense ratio of 2.39%:

However, as is the case with most closed-end funds, a significant proportion of this fund’s expenses comes from the fact that it has to pay interest on the money that it has borrowed in order to obtain leverage. If we exclude these expenses, then the LMP Capital and Income Fund had an expense ratio of 1.24%.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the LMP Capital and Income Fund is a somewhat unusual closed-end fund that boasts a portfolio that includes non-traditional fund assets such as master limited partnerships and REITs. The fund’s portfolio also looks very different from most funds’ portfolios, but this could be a good thing in the current environment. After all, this fund is overweight to energy, and energy might be one of the best-performing sectors this year due to the surge in oil prices. Overall, this fund might be worth considering for an investor who wants broader exposure to the markets than a dedicated energy fund would provide.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Amgen's Thyroid Eye Disease Data Rocks Rival Viridian, Makes It The Better Buy
data.description:
Investment Overview

Towards the end of January this year, I upgraded my rating on Amgen Inc. (AMGN) stock from a Hold (issued in a note from February 2025) to a Buy, noting that the Thousand Oaks, California-based Pharma giant:

has grown revenues at a decent clip in recent years - partly due to its acquisition of Horizon Therapeutics in October 2023, which brought an additional ~$4bn of revenues in in 2025, thanks to gout therapy Krystexxa, thyroid eye disease med Tepezza, and Uplizna, indicated for Neuromyelitis optica ("NMO").

In 2025, Amgen also secured five new FDA product approvals - and boasts no fewer than fourteen "blockbuster" (revenues >$1bn per annum) drug products.

However, I also noted that:

Immunology drug Enbrel, osteoporosis drug Prolia, bone tumor therapy Xgeva, thrombocytopenia therapy Nplate, anti-inflammatory Otezla, and kidney disease therapy Aranesp, are all set to lose — or have already lost — patent protection. In 2024, these drugs will account for ~$15bn of Amgen's annual revenues, but by 2030, I estimate, they will drive ~$5bn of revenues.

These products earned ~$13.7bn of revenues in 2025, or ~40% of Amgen's total revenues, but at a recent healthcare conference, for example, management warned that:

We continue to expect accelerated erosion in 2026 for Prolia and XGEVA, including in the first quarter because of a full year of biosimilar competition.

In summary, as older products lose patent protection, the onus falls on newer products to grow revenues, offset any losses caused by patent expiries, and keep Amgen's top (and bottom) lines growing.

While I have drawn readers' attention to Amgen's exciting pipeline GLP-1 weight loss drug MariTide in previous notes, as one reason to be invested in the Pharma - an attractive dividend currently yielding ~3% per annum is another - it's clear that Amgen's future success depends on its more recently approved assets growing market share and fending off challengers.

In that sense, news shared by Amgen yesterday concerning its Thyroid Eye Disease ("TED") drug Tepezza is worth paying attention to.

Tepezza Aces Subcutaneous Test To Underline Best In Class Status

Amgen paid ~$27.8bn to acquire Horizon Therapeutics in 2023, and last year, Krystexxa earned $1.36bn of revenues, up 15% year-on-year, and Uplizna $667m, up ~76% year-on-year. Tepezza (teprotumumab) earned $1.85bn of revenues in 2025, but this figure was down 3% year-on-year.

Meanwhile, ramping up the pressure on the drug is the fact that Viridian Therapeutics, Inc. (VRDN) hopes to secure approval for its pipeline TED asset veligrotug, which has the same administration, and a similar mechanism of action ("MoA") to Tepezza, being an intravenously administered monoclonal antibody that targets Insulin-like Growth Factor 1 Receptor ("IGF-1R"). The FDA is expected to make a decision on whether to approve veligrotug on or before June 30th this year.

Amgen has been conducting a Phase 3 study of a subcutaneously administered version of Tepezza however, using an on-body injector - similar to the way that the double-digit billion per annum selling GLP-1 weight loss drugs Zepbound or Wegovy are administered - in order to gain a further competitive advantage against its potential challengers, and announced results yesterday.

According to an Amgen press release:

The Phase 3 TEPEZZA OBI trial met its primary endpoint in moderate-to-severe active TED, showing a statistically significant and clinically meaningful 77% proptosis response rate ("PRR") during the 24-week placebo-controlled period (76.7% TEPEZZA OBI vs. 19.6% placebo [p<0.0001]). Importantly, the mean proptosis reduction, a key secondary endpoint, was -3.17 mm at week 24 (-3.17 mm TEPEZZA OBI vs. -0.80 mm placebo; p<0.0001).

Amgen also noted:

TED is a serious, progressive and potentially vision-threatening rare autoimmune disease that can cause proptosis (eye bulging), diplopia (double vision), eye pain, redness and swelling.

Tepezza as an intravenously administered therapy was first approved in 2020 based on data from its pivotal OPTIC study - as per Horizon's press release at the time:

The OPTIC study found that significantly more patients treated with Tepezza (82.9%) had a meaningful improvement in proptosis (≥ 2 mm) as compared with placebo patients (9.5%) (p˂0.001) without deterioration in the fellow eye at Week 24. Additional secondary endpoints were also met, including a change from baseline of at least one grade in diplopia (double vision) in 67.9% of patients receiving Tepezza compared to 28.6% of patients receiving placebo (p=0.001) at Week 24.

Amgen management discussed the subcutaneous opportunity during its recent Q&A at the Leerink Healthcare conference, noting its belief that market penetration is "still only single digit," and that a subQ version of its drug will "help in unlocking that chronic patient population." Management also noted that "we envision IV continuing."

Although seemingly slightly down on efficacy versus IV administration, Wall Street sounded a positive note - Leerink analysts are now forecasting for at least $2bn in Tepezza revenues this year.

Amgen Stays One Step Ahead In TED Market - But For How Long (& What Is The Market Opportunity)?

Despite its data being well received, Amgen is down ~3.5% on a five-day basis, and down ~6% on a one-month basis (it is up ~10% on a 1-year basis).

The share price of its potential new rival in the TED space, Viridian Therapeutics, however, has fallen from a three-year high of ~$33 per share in late January this year, to ~$14 at the time of writing, down >50% year-to-date, and >50% in the past month.

The Amgen data seems to have completed a "double whammy" of bad news for Viridian. The company is developing a second TED targeting drug besides veligrotug, elegrobart, which is a subcutaneously administered IGF-1R targeting monoclonal antibody.

Viridian shared results from its Phase 3 REVEAL-1 study of elegrobart at the end of March, noting that:

Elegrobart Q4W and Q8W achieved clinically meaningful 54% and 63% PRR (proptosis responder rate) versus 18% placebo at week 24.

Q4W means dosed every four weeks, and Q8W every 8 weeks, and clearly, the efficacy achieved does not match subcutaneous Tepezza, although we may note that in Amgen's study, participants received TEPEZZA every two weeks for a total of 12 injections.

Wall Street dumped Viridian stock after the data were shared, having expected a >50% placebo-adjusted PPR, and dumped stock once again yesterday after Amgen's Phase 3 Tepezza readout, but could this be an overreaction?

Veligrotug achieved a 48% placebo adjusted PRR in its pivotal study, making it more comparable to Tepezza IV, efficacy wise, plus the company will soon share more data in relation to elegrobart from a second Phase 3 study, which seems likely to report around the same time as the "PDUFA" (prescription drug user fee act) date for veligrotug i.e. late June.

The reality is that both Amgen and Viridian may face more competition in the TED marketplace before long - according to Viridian's 2025 annual report / 10-K submission:

  • Immunovant (IMVT) is developing a subcutaneous formulation of batoclimab in phase 3 studies in patients with active TED.
  • Roche (RHHBY) is developing satralizumab (Enspryng), an anti-1L-6R that is subcutaneously delivered and was studied in two phase 3 trial in patients with TED, one of which (SatraGO-1) failed to meet its primary endpoint.
  • Sling Therapeutics, Inc. is developing linsitinib, a small molecule IGF-1R inhibitor currently being evaluated in an ongoing phase 2b/3 study in patients with active TED and currently has plans to initiate a phase 3 trial.
  • Novartis (formerly Tourmaline Bio) (NVS) is developing TOUR006, a subcutaneously delivered anti-IL-6, in an ongoing phase 2b clinical trial in patients with active TED.
  • Lassen Therapeutics is developing LASN01, an intravenously delivered anti-1L-11R, which has completed a phase 2 study in active TED.
  • Alumis (ACELYRIN, INC.) (ALMS) is developing lonigutamab (VB-421), a subcutaneously delivered anti-IGF-1R in TED. As of January 2026, further development plans for lonigutamab are under evaluation.

While Amgen and Viridian remain the leaders of the pack, with, for example, Roche having already experienced a study fail, and Alumis potentially likely to cease development of lonigutamab altogether, it does not seem too unlikely that by 2030, there could three or four TED therapies on the market, all with a similar MoA and with a convenient subcutaneous administration.

Is the market opportunity in play genuinely large enough to support long-term, multi-billion dollar sales for two or three assets? Viridian has speculated (in a corporate presentation) that the TED market comprises ~190k patients, and when launched, Tepezza apparently commanded a price of ~$200k for a six-month course of therapy ($14,900-per-vial list price).

A theoretical market opportunity of ~$38bn, then - large enough to support several competing therapies - but that goes against the reality that Tepezza - currently the only approved therapy on the market - is experiencing falling sales.

As we can see above, Viridian sees TED as a potential ~$525k per patient per annum market, and has had no issue recruiting patients for its studies, but should veligrotug secure approval, will patients - and insurers - be put off by the high price of the therapy?

Something certainly seems to have prevented Tepezza from growing revenues in 2025, however Amgen has previously suggested it is product awareness that is holding sales back, as opposed to cost or lack of patients, and has committed to launching its product in new markets, and building on ~25k patients treated to date (figure quoted in Q4 earnings call), while it is also focused on development of a Phase 2 stage candidate, AMG 732.

Personally, I believe the presence of two TED drugs on the market could be a net positive for both Amgen and Viridian, as it will create greater overall awareness, and if both companies do successfully launch a SubQ version of their product, it may allow them to lower prices and engage more patients who otherwise may not have considered therapy.

Apparently, Tepezza's patent protection runs into the middle of the next decade, so there are few concerns on that score, and with Viridian's market cap being only $1.42bn at the time of writing, frankly, if the company achieves "blockbuster" (>$1bn per annum) revenues by 2030, its valuation could double or even triple in value, in my view, with the forward price to sales ratio still remaining well below 5x (my rule of thumb for valuing a commercial stage Pharma, provided it is not too indebted, and Viridian reported nearly $900m cash as of the end of Q425).

Concluding Thoughts: I Make Amgen A Solid Buy, Viridian Too Risky A Buy

Clearly, Tepezza is only a small part of Amgen's overall business, representing ~5% of the Pharma's revenues in 2025 - six Amgen products earned more revenues last year, and some of these have exciting growth potential - cholesterol drug Repatha earned $2.96bn, up 33% year-on-year, and osteoporosis med Evenity $2.1bn, also up 33%, while Blinctyto and Tezspire (luekemia, asthma), earned $1.6bn and $1.5bn, up respectively 28% and 51% year-on-year.

Amgen's guidance for 2026 is for $37bn - $38.4bn, with non-GAAP EPS of $21.6 - $23, and total Capex of ~$2.6bn. That represents ~7.5% annual revenue growth, with EPS growth being broadly flat. Forward price to sales ("P/S") ratio is therefore ~5.4x, and forward price to earnings ("P/E") ratio ~17x - right about average for the "Big Pharma" sector, I'd say.

Amgen carries a substantial amount of debt on its balance sheet - its interest expense in 2025 was >$3bn, albeit down from >$4bn in 2023, but the company has an exciting and diverse pipeline of candidates, some highlighted below:

As shown in the table, I suspect Mari-Tide is Amgen's most significant pipeline product, and obesity study readouts due early 2027 will be a crucial factor in determining the Pharma's long-term share price performance, and I see enough potential to wonder if its past 10-year share price performance of >122% can be repeated across the next decade.

Perhaps, due to patent expiry issues, that is too much to expect, but as a bona-fide member of the "Big Pharma" club, with a strong pipeline, I see few drawbacks to holding Amgen stock and forecast for revenues of ~$55bn (CAGR of ~8%) by 2030, with Tepezza achieving a figure of ~$4bn per annum.

Where does that leave Viridian? As mentioned, if the company secures approval for veligrotug in June, it's possible we'll see an uplift in the share price, and I'd also remind readers that data from a second Phase 3 study of elegrobart is due mid-year, which could change the market's perception of the asset.

That potentially makes Viridian an intriguing, "buy the dip," contrarian trade at this time, as I concur with management that the market opportunity in TED is under-exploited, and that healthy competition between these two pharmas (I don't expect approval for a third TED asset within a two-to-three year period, unless it is elegrobart), will actually help to grow the market.

In the final analysis, however, there are several weighty factors that make me hesitant to recommend Viridian, while I remain bullish on Amgen. For example, the fact that Viridian is bringing an IV therapy to market just as Amgen looks to move into the SubQ market.

Also, the doubts concerning elegrobart's efficacy as a SubQ therapy, and Viridian's lack of experience in commercial markets that Amgen is now well-versed in, after two full years selling Tepezza, plus the upcoming competition, which I regard as more likely to impact Viridian than Amgen, again due to Viridian's lack of experience and more limited budget. Viridian may boast ~$888m cash, but it reported net income of $(343m) last year, and $(270m) in 2024, so its funding runway could be exhausted in a couple of years' time.

As such, if you are an investor looking for exposure to a TED market that research suggests may be under-exploited, my recommendation would be Amgen - holding Viridian feels a little too risky today, but may be worth reassessing once we have seen the second Phase 3 elegrobart data set, likely in a few month's time.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Micron Technology: Crowded Bet On A Cyclical High
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Micron is one of three global-scale memory manufacturers (controlling ~95% of DRAM production alongside Samsung and SK Hynix) that produces DRAM, NAND, and high-bandwidth memory for data centers, PCs, smartphones, AI accelerators, etc. The stock has surged dramatically on AI super-cycle expectations, with consensus pointing to sold-out HBM capacity through 2026 and structurally higher memory content per AI server.

It is my view that the memory cycle is at or near its peak.

I rate MU a Sell for three reasons. First, the indicators of a cyclical top are present: parabolic contract pricing, OEM inventory pull-forwards, and downstream demand destruction in consumer devices. Second, recent rollouts of compression technologies (viz., Google's TurboQuant) reduce memory intensity per AI workload, which means fewer chips will be needed per workload. Third, the supply-side response to extraordinary margins has created a prisoner's dilemma in which each manufacturer rationally expands capacity, collectively causing oversupply.

Valuation

Micron's trailing P/E is ~17x. Its forward P/E, based on the next twelve months consensus EPS of ~$57, is ~6.5x. Both look reasonable. For a cyclical name at peak earnings, however, this warrants a more careful consideration.

The 2018 precedent is meaningful here. At the last memory cycle peak, MU traded at a trailing P/E of approximately 3x before crashing roughly 50%. Today's 17x also looks low compared to average Q1 2025's ~28x. But that compression is often a signal, especially in the case of cyclicals, that earnings are inflating the denominator. As more peak-cycle quarters roll into the TTM calculation, the trailing P/E will compress further toward the forward P/E of ~6x. That is the zone where prior cycles have trapped many buyers.

The peak is visible in the pricing data. DRAM contract prices have surged 45-50% QoQ in Q4 2025, with a further 60% climb in Q1 2026, while spot prices on some DRAM chips have risen 300-1,000% from early 2025 levels. OEMs responded by shipping aggressively in Q4 2025 and Q1 2026 to get ahead of further increases, pulling forward demand that would have supported volumes later in the year. HP's CFO disclosed that memory and storage climbed from 15-18% of its PC bill of materials to approximately 35%. Samsung raised Galaxy S26 prices 5-10% at launch.

The demand destruction is already visible. Gartner estimates a 130% surge in combined DRAM and SSD prices by the end of 2026, driving PC shipments down 10.4% and smartphone shipments down 8.4%. IDC estimates global smartphone shipments to decline 12.9% in 2026, representing the sharpest single-year drop in over a decade. Counterpoint estimates a 12% decline, with volumes falling to their lowest since 2013. In 2018, similar conditions preceded a 50% drop in memory prices.

MU's financial position is strong. Free cash flow hit a quarterly record of $6.9 billion in FQ2 2026. Management raised the dividend by 30%. But these are conditions that exist at every cyclical peak. It does not necessarily mean that those numbers are sustainable.

Catalysts

First, AI capex normalization. Current guidance assumes near-linear demand growth for AI infrastructure. If aggregate spending slows from "explosive" to merely "strong" while multiples still reflect the super-cycle, the stock derates. TrendForce's base case (60% probability) is that DRAM prices peak in Q1 2026, with relief beginning in Q3 2026 and normalization by Q1-Q2 2027.

Second, memory-saving technologies. Google's TurboQuant, released on March 25, 2026, compresses LLM key-value cache memory by at least 6x with zero accuracy loss. Memory chip stocks fell 5-7% on the announcement. TurboQuant is training-free, plug-and-play, and applicable to any existing model. The barrier to adoption is near zero, and the efficiency gain follows a one-way ratchet, meaning that once inference operators serve the same workload with 6x less KV cache memory, there is no incentive to revert. The Jevons paradox (cheaper inference expands total workloads enough to offset the efficiency gain) is still relevant here but nevertheless uncertain. Consensus assumes current memory intensity per model run persists. It most likely will not.

Third, the supply-side response. Tight supply has been a key ingredient in Micron's 196% YoY revenue growth and 74.4% gross margins in FQ2 2026. But each of the three manufacturers independently faces a dominant strategy to expand when margins are this high. Micron has raised FY2026 capex to over $25 billion from an initial $18 billion. Samsung and SK Hynix are investing in parallel. The players are individually rational but collectively destructive. This is a key dynamic that plays out in every industry cycles.

For now, AI data center demand more than offsets consumer weakness. Cloud and Core Data Center accounted for approximately $13.4 billion, or 56% of FQ2 2026 revenue, while Mobile and Client contributed $7.71 billion (32%) and Automotive and Embedded roughly 11%. The bear case is therefore not that consumer destruction overwhelms AI demand now, but that when AI capex normalizes and compression reduces memory intensity, the collapse in consumer volumes (e.g., smartphones down 12%, PCs down 5-10%) removes the cushion that would otherwise absorb the excess capacity. New fab capacity arriving in 2027-2028 into that environment will likely tip the balance faster than consensus assumes.

Fourth, macro and positioning risk. MU's 5Y beta is 1.61. The S&P 500 recently entered correction territory, falling approximately 10% from its January peak by March 30, driven primarily by tariffs, the Iran conflict (oil briefly exceeded $100 per barrel), and a surprise loss of 92,000 jobs in February. The Fed also held rates at 3.50-3.75%. There is visible stress in private credit markets. BofA described a rotation away from technology, noting that five of the Magnificent Seven underperformed the S&P 500 in 2025. High-beta names will experience sharp drops in price if macroeconomic conditions continue to deteriorate and passive flows reverse.

Risks

The biggest risk to the sell thesis is if the AI super-cycle lasts longer and runs stronger. If hyperscalers, sovereign AI programs, and enterprises keep ramping up GPU deployments, Micron could continue beating expectations and sustain elevated margins. Still, I think that the stock's current pricing already embeds most of this optimism.

Second, Micron's cost structure is better than in past cycles. The company may sustain higher margins in a downturn than history implies. I believe the magnitude of current peak-cycle profitability relative to normalized returns still sets up an unfavorable risk-reward from here.

Third, the Jevons paradox could dominate the compression narrative. If TurboQuant and similar technologies make inference sufficiently cheap, total AI workloads could expand enough to increase aggregate memory demand despite lower intensity per workload. This is plausible but requires demand growth that offsets both the efficiency gains and the incoming supply additions. I see this working in the long term, but not necessarily in the short to medium term.

Conclusion

Micron is operationally strong—that much is indisputable. What is, however, is whether its numbers represent a sustainable baseline or a cyclical peak.

The evidence points to the latter. DRAM contract prices have risen at a pace not seen since the last cycle top. Consumer device shipments are collapsing under the weight of memory costs. OEMs are pulling forward inventory. The P/E looks cheap, but it looked cheap at 3x in 2018 as well. Compression technologies that reduce memory intensity are deployable today with near-zero adoption barriers. And the supply-side prisoner's dilemma is already in motion, with over $25 billion in Micron capex alone committed to capacity that will arrive into a market where the non-AI demand base (i.e., smartphones, PCs, etc.) is already shrinking.

I rate MU a Sell. At current levels, the downside risks are asymmetrically underpriced. I would revisit this rating if a) the share price fell to a forward P/E in the low teens on conservative mid-cycle EPS (i.e., well below the current ~$57 NTM consensus), or b) evidence accumulates that AI-driven memory demand can sustain today's margins across a full cycle. Neither condition holds today.

data.title: Essential Properties: Buy-The-Dip Opportunity
data.description:
Patience is the name of the game, especially when it comes to compounded returns based on dividends and growth. Dividend stocks may not be hot conversation topics at cocktail parties, especially when compared to trendy names in cryptocurrencies like Bitcoin (BTC-USD) or AI Stocks like NVIDIA (NVDA).

However, investors who ignore dividends do so at their own peril as those income streams have a cumulative effect that materially adds to total returns. This brings me to Essential Properties Realty Trust (EPRT), which I last covered a while back in February 2024, highlighting its quality properties and positioning in the underserved middle market.

Since then, EPRT has delivered an impressive 37% total return, far surpassing the 29% rise in the S&P 500 (SPY) over the same timeframe, as shown below.

At the current price of $30.85, EPRT trades well below its 52-week high of $34.73, particularly after the recent dip in price. It also comes with a reasonable forward P/FFO of 14.2 and a 4.0% dividend yield, as shown below.

In this article, I revisit EPRT including recent business results, and discuss why the recent dip creates a great buying opportunity on this growth REIT, so let's get started!

Why EPRT?

Essential Properties Realty Trust is a net lease REIT focused on single-tenant, service-oriented properties across the U.S. It primarily partners with middle-market operators through sale-leaseback transactions, providing capital while securing long-term, triple-net leases.

This niche positioning has allowed EPRT to carve out a differentiated role as a capital provider rather than a traditional landlord. This means that EPRT emphasizes granular deal sourcing and relationship-driven origination with local businesses.

This, combined with its smaller size, has translated into an outsized total return of 222% compared to net lease peers Realty Income (O), NNN REIT (NNN), W. P. Carey (WPC), and VICI Properties (VICI) over the past 10 years, as shown below.

At present, EPRT has 2,300 properties with an emphasis on high unit-level rent coverage. It gets unit-level financial reporting from 99.2% of tenants (as percentage of ABR), enabling management to keep a close tab on portfolio health. The portfolio is geared toward essential services that are e-commerce and recession-resilient.

As shown below, auto service, quick service restaurants, childcare, healthcare, and car washes make up the top 5 industries, representing 54% of ABR.

EPRT continued to deliver solid results during Q4 2025, with AFFO per share rising by 9% YoY to $0.49, landing at the high end of expectations. This was driven by stable tenant performance including rent escalator and accretive investments.

During Q4, EPRT deployed $296 million in capital at a high weighted average cash yield of 7.7%. This compares favorably to peers O and NNN, which saw initial cash yields of 7.1% to 7.3% on investments made during the same quarter.

Importantly, these acquisitions came with unit-level rent coverage of 4.7x, sitting higher than the 3.6x portfolio average. 85% of these investments were sourced through existing relationships, reflecting the value of EPRT's relationship-driven sourcing model. Moreover, occupancy remained very high at 99.7% and under 1% of EPRT's ABR is under credit watchlist, marking a sequential QoQ improvement.

Looking ahead to Q1 results and beyond, I expect to see continued strong growth through acquisitions. This is supported by management's guidance for $1 billion to $1.4 billion in acquisitions this year. Management has noted that growth is not constrained by opportunity (of which there are many), but rather by the objective of generating consistent risk-adjusted returns over time.

At present, cap rates on opportunities remain stable in the high-7% range with modest compression not expected until later this year. Notably, management has noted that competitive pressure on deals have not intensified as previously expected.

EPRT maintains a strong balance sheet with BBB-/BBB credit ratings from S&P and Fitch. This is supported by a low net debt to EBITDA ratio of 3.8x and $1.4 billion in liquidity. This gives EPRT plenty of flexibility to fund investments without heavy reliance on equity issuance.

EPRT currently yields a respectable 4%. While this isn't high compared to O and NNN, the dividend comes with a lower payout ratio of just 59%. This leaves plenty of room for internal funding of growth with retained cash flows and future dividend growth. EPRT has a 5-year dividend CAGR of 5.4% and 7 consecutive years of dividend growth.

I continue to find value in EPRT at the current price of $30.85, particularly after the recent dip in price. At this price, it has a forward P/FFO of 14.2, sitting well below its historical P/FFO of 17.3, as shown below.

EPRT's valuation sits just above O's forward P/FFO of 13.9 and above NNN's P/FFO of 12.1. While I view NNN as being the value play in the net lease REIT sector right now, EPRT is my top growth pick at a reasonable valuation.

At the present price, I don't think the market fully appreciate EPRT's high FFO/share growth potential in the 6% to 9% range. This, combined with plenty of retained cashflow and a 4% dividend yield, could result in continued total returns in the teens from here.

Risks to EPRT include those on tenant watchlist. Tenant bankruptcies could result in property vacancies and downtime, which could impact near-term results. This could be exacerbated by higher oil prices and inflation. Increased competition for deals could compress EPRT's investment yields on acquisitions. Plus, higher interest rates could raise EPRT's interest rates and cost of funding.

Investor Takeaway

The recent pullback in Essential Properties Realty Trust presents a compelling entry point for long-term income and growth investors. EPRT has solid relationship-driven acquisition growth and strong portfolio fundamentals. It also has high occupancy and a conservative balance sheet.

EPRT's lower payout ratio versus peers and consistent AFFO growth support continued dividend increases and reinvestment opportunities. As such, its 4% dividend yield positions it as a total return story rather than a pure income play.

Lastly, the recent dip in price has created an undervalued opportunity on the stock, while it trades below historical valuation. This sets up potential for total returns in the teens. For these reasons, I believe EPRT stands out as a disciplined, growth-oriented REIT capable of delivering attractive compounded returns over time.

data.title: Amcor: Dividend Aristocrat With A Great Yield And Below Fair Value
data.description:
My Investing Philosophy

I like dividend aristocrats and kings because these companies exhibit a commitment to rising dividends, and I like rising dividends. I also like to find companies that fit these definitions that are out of favor and selling at a discount to their respective fair values.

I use multiple sources of analysis and expected growth rates as well as articles, opinions and corporate data releases/presentations. But I prefer to discern from all that for myself what I really expect from a company and then plug my assumptions into my models to find what I believe to be the true fair value of a company based upon discounted future cash flows and my own required rate of return.

I like to add stocks to my portfolio of companies that I expect to have competitive advantages, good management and strong balance sheets combined with a business model that has weathered multiple periods of economic and geopolitical turmoil only to emerge stronger, demonstrating resilience. To understand my background, experience and more about my investing philosophy, check out my profile.

I believe that Amcor (AMCR) fits that description and I will use this article to explain why.

Company Background

Amcor is a packaging company, in the same industry as Sonoco (SON) which I wrote about in June of last year. At the time of that article, SON was trading at ~$44.26/share with a dividend yield of about 5%. Today that stock is trading at $54.84 and yielding 4.5%. In less than a year I have a total return of 27.5%. In that article I forecasted an average annual total return of 27.8% over the next five years. So far, so good on that one. In case you missed it, I thought I’d write about another company in the same industry with good prospects. I remain long SON.

Amcor designs and manufactures packaging for multiple industries such as food, pharmaceutical, medical and consumer products. It emphasizes lightweight, reusable and recyclable packaging. It is the outcome of a merger between Australia-based Amcor and U.S.-based Bemis in 2019.

The company closed on an acquisition of Berry Global in April 2025 and is ahead of schedule in its quest to leverage synergies to cut costs. On an adjusted basis (removing the one-time costs associated with the acquisition), both margins and EPS are showing significant improvements.

Humans will continue to buy stuff, and it will come in packages. Of that I am certain. I also believe that the benefits that can be achieved through economies of scale will be more important to the future of the packaging industry than AI. Amcor is a leader in terms of innovation and size and I believe that will enable it to capture further cost efficiencies.

I recommend reading an article by Juan Ignacio Orlando, here on Seeking Alpha, for an in-depth description of Amcor and its operations. He does an excellent job of explaining how Amcor’s size and footprint across 400 locations in 40 countries provide it competitive advantages. His article goes deeper into the operations side while I intend to focus more on the valuation.

Earnings

Amcor’s fiscal year ends in June, so the most recent quarterly earnings report on February 3 was for its second quarter. You can find the quarterly earnings presentation here.

For the quarter, net sales increased by 68% to $545 million, primarily as a result of its acquisition of Berry Global. Management’s ability to capture synergies from the combination will prove crucial to improving EPS (earnings per share). So far, the progress has been very positive.

The packaging industry is resilient but cyclical. It is best to buy when a company in this industry is out of favor and only when the company has exhibited the ability to come out of down cycles in better shape than when entering. Investor concerns over the acquisition have created just such an opportunity with Amcor.

Large, multinational companies, like PepsiCo (PEP) and McDonald’s (MCD), need packaging to be consistent in design and quality across their entire global footprint. There is an extensive process involved for one of these companies to move packaging from one company to another. First, it must find a packaging company that can serve it on a global scale, of which there are very few that can match Amcor’s global footprint. Second, it would need to find a company that can also produce the multiple types of packaging requirements for different products. Again, Amcor is well-positioned to address such issues better than smaller competitors.

Finally, economy of scale like Amcor has achieved allow it to achieve greater concessions on pricing of raw materials from suppliers than its smaller competitors, which can enable Amcor to compete on price better than many of its competitors. Size matters in this business.

The earnings history shown below displays a mostly positive picture from the EPS predictability perspective. Revenue reports have missed but continue to show mostly positive improvement on a Y/O/Y (year/over/year) basis, especially since the acquisition.

Projected consensus EPS exhibit strong growth going forward, while I believe these estimates will probably be adjusted down as the actual year-end reporting periods approach, it is important to remember that this is the game Wall Street analysts play every year; extreme optimism at the beginning with downward adjustments throughout the year to make beating estimates easier. So I think the growth rate will be lower but still very positive.

Growth Prospects

Speaking of growth, the growth grade shown in the table below shows an expectation of average growth, which once the first year of the Berry acquisition has passed, I believe will be accurate. My forecast is for long-term EPS growth of ~7% average annually over the next five years.

However, with the expanded footprint, capabilities and customers resulting from the acquisition, Amcor has some interesting growth prospects available to it, as I explained earlier in the article.

Management projects 10-15% growth in EPS, as shown in the graphic below from the Amcor quarterly earnings presentation. That may be a bit optimistic, in my opinion. But management is always presenting an optimistic view of the future, no matter which company it is.

You should note that management also projects a future dividend yield 3-4%. I assume that means that dividend growth will be slower than EPS growth rather than a dividend cut since management also reiterates its commitment to continue to grow the dividend.

Profitability

The profitability grade shown below is slightly above average (B-) and I would agree with that assessment. Two metrics I look at closely are return on total capital, which is about average for the sector, and cash from operations, which is very high relative to the sector. I like companies that can generate strong cash flows. That usually helps protect the dividend.

Management at Amcor is focused on extracting cost savings from synergies that can be identified from the acquisition. Reducing headcount in back-office operations is only one area. Eliminating less efficient facilities where those operations duplicate efforts by other, more efficient facilities is another. And cross-selling both new and existing customers with products that were not previously available to them from one of the two organizations pre-merger can create more demand and improve efficiencies by increasing capacity utilization.

The company projects total cumulative savings of $650 million through 2028. That all flows down to the bottom line, so improved EPS should be good during that period.

Balance Sheet

The balance sheet currently has more debt than I would like, but management appears to be committed to using free cash flow to reduce leverage to a more reasonable level. Again, this is something to keep an eye on over the coming quarters to ensure that management is making the necessary progress.

If we assume management is up to the task, which I believe it is, then the deleveraging process should allow the company to maintain an investment grade credit rating.

Dividend

The dividend grades do not fully credit the company’s full history because of the lack of long-term history for the combined company. If we assume the dividend track record of Bemis as the basis for the dividend growth history, we find that the company has a record of 42 consecutive years of dividend growth, making it a dividend aristocrat. The current company, post-merger, has only been around for 7 years, thus the 6 years of dividend growth displayed below.

The dividend growth rate has been only 2% over the past five years, but I expect that rate to increase, although not as much as the growth rate for EPS as the company gradually brings the payout ratio down closer to 60%.

I really like the 6.5% current yield. Such a yield is hard to find among dividend aristocrats. I also like the prospects of a rising dividend. Both allow me to remain relaxed while I wait for the appreciation I expect to occur, as I’ll discuss in the valuation section later.

Valuation

I completely agree with the valuation grade below (A). By my calculations, Amcor is trading at a discount to fair market value of about 15-16%. I expect the stock to revert to its true market value over time as future reported results display the modest, but predictable growth I expect.

Let’s apply a couple of different valuation methods to see what AMCR should be worth going forward. First, if I look at AMCR through the lens of my DDM (Dividend Discount Model), I get a very favorable outcome of a $47.27 fair market value.

For the discount rate I use in this model, I substitute my required rate of return for the cost of capital. That personalizes the formula to help me identify companies that meet my criteria for the long term, rather than a valuation that is subject to change with movements in interest rates. I also vary the discount rate from one industry to another to keep my expectations realistic (I expect higher rates of return from technology than utilities, for example). AMCOR is in the materials sector, for which I require a 10% annual compound total annual return on investment. So, my formula looks like this: D/(M-X)=V or $2.60/(.10-.045)=$47.27.

Where:

D = Current Dividend

M = Discount Rate

X = Expected Future Growth of Dividend

The next step is to determine the average annual increase to the stock’s value as it returns to fair value. the Excel formula is: =((F/C)^(1/5))-1 ; or

=((47.27/39.93)^(1/5)-1 = 3.4%

Where F is the fair value and C is the current stock price.

Now we add the average annual valuation gain of 3.4% to the dividend yield of 6.5% and the expected growth in EPS of 7% to get a CAGR of 16.9%.

Another way to value AMCR is to add the dividend, expected growth in earnings, and the expected growth from the multiple expansion. This last piece requires a formula to calculate the annual compound expected growth in the PE from the current level to the historical average (reversion to the mean). Again, the Excel formula is: =((F/C)^(1/5))-1

Where:

F = Future PE ratio

C = Current PE ratio

5 = Number of years expected to revert to mean

or

=((12/10.05)^(1/5))-1=3.6%

This means that if the PE ratio rises over the next 5 years to a reasonable average of 12 from the current level of 10.05, the average annual increase in value due to PE expansion will be 3.6%.

Then we add the current dividend of 6.5% and the expected earnings growth rate of 7% to 3.6%, and we get an expected annual compound growth rate of return equal to 17.1%.

The fair value under this method is $47.64. (PE of 12 multiplied by the forward EPS of $3.97).

Finally, one other way to calculate the expected annual total return is to estimate the future EPS five years out multiplied by the expected PE of 12 to determine a target price for the stock and then add the estimated cumulative dividends we expect to collect over the five-year period. That gives us the total expected future value of our investment. Then, of course, we use another formula to determine the average expected total annual return.

Future estimated EPS in five years is calculated by assuming the average dividend growth rate of 4.5% over 5 periods, and we get an expected future dividend of $3.10 in year 5 and a cumulative total of $14.22 collected over the five-year period. Using the 7% expected growth rate for EPS, we find the expected EPS in year 5 to be $5.20/share. Multiply that by the expected PE of 12, and we get a future price target of $62.45. Then we add the future price to the cumulative dividends for a total of $76.67. That amount minus the current price (our original investment) of $39.93 results in a total gain of $36.74, or about 92%.

To calculate the compound annual total return rate we use the formula: =(($76.67/$39.93)^(1/5)-1, or 13.9%.

I like the first two results better but even at 13.9%, the investment achieves my minimum required rate of return, so I’ll take it.

Potential Risks

Of course, if the acquisition integration does not go as well as management expects, the analyst forecasts will prove to be too optimistic, and growth will not be as robust. I have tried to adjust my expectations to accommodate this risk by reducing my own projection by nearly half what the consensus is. Could even I be too optimistic? Of course, I could. Company managers are almost always overly optimistic about the acquisition they just made. I may need to temper my expectations in the future, and will do so in another article, if results do not show improvement over the next two quarters.

If the wars in Ukraine and the Middle East spread to encompass and include more nations, global economies could slow more than expected, resulting in the potential erosion of expected sales and earnings. This could also disrupt supply chains and demand.

If inflation rises due to many possible factors, not the least of which is the cost of energy, it could dampen demand globally, which could result in lower sales and earnings. It could also keep interest rates higher than expected, which could dampen multiple expansion. Again, I tried to adjust consensus expectations by lowering my future PE expectation from 15 to 12. That may not be enough in the short term, but I think it will prove conservative over the longer term.

Most of these concerns apply to almost every company in varying degrees, with the exception of the first one, which is company-specific.

Conclusion

A 14% to 17% total average compound annual return over the next five years is enticing to me and collecting the 6.5% dividend yield (and rising each year) makes the wait very comfortable. I’ll let the cash keep piling up until I find another bargain to add to my portfolio. I just love the power of compounding over time!

I rate Amcor a BUY for investors seeking current income and double-digit total returns.
